content.full_text: March 22, 1976 Don P. Bonfa City Attorney P.O. Box 190 Huntington Beach CA 92648 Dear Mr. Bonfa: This letter responds to your letter of March 16, 1976, concerning the impact of Buckley v. Valeo on the Political Reform Act. Although the Commission has not fully explored all the implications of the decision, the staff has identified the following three areas as topics that are particularly affected by Buckley: (1) Expenditure Limitations The Political Reform Act establishes limitations on the amounts that can be spent to campaign for or against a statewide candidate, Government Code Sections 85100-85108, and a statewide measure, Sections 85300-85305. Expenditure limitations in both areas appear to be foreclosed by the Buckley decision. For your interest, I am enclosing a copy of the return filed by the commission in Citizens for Jobs and Energy v. Fair Political Practices Commission, an original writ proceeding in the California Supreme Court that challenged the constitutionality of the ballot measure expenditure limitations. In light of the Buckley decision, the Commission is not enforcing the provisions of Sections 85300-85305. The Political Reform Act also limits the amounts that can be spent to circulate signature petitions so that a measure will be placed on the ballot, Sections 85200-85203. These limitations are the subject of litigation currently before the Superior Court of Sacramento County, and we are awaiting judicial determination of the controversy. (2) The meaning of "Expenditure" and "Contribution" The Commission is struggling to narrow the definition of "expenditure" and "contribution" so that the terms will not be overbroad or unconstitutionally vague when applied to organizations that carry on other activities in addition to campaigning for candidates or measures. This page consists almost entirely of typewritten notes in Spanish, with some English words and numbers scattered throughout. The content appears to be a detailed record or summary of some kind, possibly related to a meeting, report, or discussion. Due to the quality of the scan, some parts of the text are partially illegible, but the majority is still readable. The notes contain references to "expediente" (file), "comisión" (commission), "asesoría" (advisory), "asesoría técnica" (technical advisory), and other terms that suggest an official or administrative context. There are also numbers such as 82100-82108, which may refer to specific files or documents.
content.full_text_markdown:
March 22, 1976

Don P. Bonfa  
City Attorney  
P.O. Box 190  
Huntington Beach CA 92648

Dear Mr. Bonfa:

This letter responds to your letter of March 16, 1976, concerning the impact of Buckley v. Valeo on the Political Reform Act. Although the Commission has not fully explored all the implications of the decision, the staff has identified the following three areas as topics that are particularly affected by Buckley:

(1) Expenditure Limitations

The Political Reform Act establishes limitations on the amounts that can be spent to campaign for or against a statewide candidate, Government Code Sections 85100-85108, and a statewide measure, Sections 85300-85305. Expenditure limitations in both areas appear to be foreclosed by the Buckley decision. For your interest, I am enclosing a copy of the return filed by the commission in Citizens for Jobs and Energy v. Fair Political Practices Commission, an original writ proceeding in the California Supreme Court that challenged the constitutionality of the ballot measure expenditure limitations. In light of the Buckley decision, the Commission is not enforcing the provisions of Sections 85300-85305. The Political Reform Act also limits the amounts that can be spent to circulate signature petitions so that a measure will be placed on the ballot, Sections 85200-85203. These limitations are the subject of litigation currently before the Superior Court of Sacramento County, and we are awaiting judicial determination of the controversy.

(2) The meaning of "Expenditure" and "Contribution"

The Commission is struggling to narrow the definition of "expenditure" and "contribution" so that the terms will not be overbroad or unconstitutionally vague when applied to organizations that carry on other activities in addition to campaigning for candidates or measures.

This page consists almost entirely of typewritten notes in Spanish, with some English words and numbers scattered throughout. The content appears to be a detailed record or summary of some kind, possibly related to a meeting, report, or discussion. Due to the quality of the scan, some parts of the text are partially illegible, but the majority is still readable.

The notes contain references to "expediente" (file), "comisión" (commission), "asesoría" (advisory), "asesoría técnica" (technical advisory), and other terms that suggest an official or administrative context. There are also numbers such as 82100-82108, which may refer to specific files or documents.

Some of the visible text includes:

- "expediente"
- "asesoría"
- "asesoría técnica"

- "

(3) Minor party exemptions from disclosure requirements.

The Commission is fashioning a mechanism for providing an exemption from disclosure requirements for parties that can demonstrate a "reasonable probability of harassment" as a result of forced disclosure. Last year, the Commission refused to grant an exemption to the Socialist Workers Party but is reexamining its policy to comply with the standards set forth in Buckley.

The Political Reform Act does not limit contributions, and, consequently, this letter does not address the issues raised by contribution limitations.

I hope this information will be of assistance. Please contact me if you have additional questions.

Sincerely,

Natalie E. West,
Staff Counsel

NEW: pvp

The image is a typed letter with several lines of text, but the majority of the content is reversed and mostly illegible due to the quality of the scan. However, some partially visible words and numbers include:

- "SPEF Committee"
- "Marie E. Merl"
- "Labs 140"
- "March 25, 1936"
- "Don Bouis"

The remainder of the text is mostly reversed and cannot be reliably transcribed accurately. If you have another scan or a higher quality scan of this document, please provide it for better results.

**OFFICE OF CITY ATTORNEY**
P.O. BOX 190
HUNTINGTON BEACH
CALIFORNIA 92648

DON P. BONFA
CITY ATTORNEY

TELEPHONE (714) 536-5555

16 March 1976

Fair Political Practices Commission
State of California
Post Office Box 807
Sacramento, California 95804

Dear Sirs:

In light of the recent U.S. Supreme Court decision, *Buckley v. Valeo*, would you please send us your analysis of this case as it affects the Political Reform Act of 1974, Government Code Sections 81000 et seq. We are particularly interested in the areas of contributions and expense limitations.

Thank you.

Very truly yours,

[Signature]
DON P. BONFA
City Attorney

Box 190 Huntington Beach CA 92648 Dear Mr. Bonfa: This letter responds to your letter of March 16, 1976, concerning the impact of Buckley v. Valeo on the Political Reform Act. Although the Commission has not fully explored all the implications of the decision, the staff has identified the following three areas as topics that are particularly affected by Buckley: (1) Expenditure Limitations The Political Reform Act establishes limitations on the amounts that can be spent to campaign for or against a statewide candidate, Government Code Sections 85100-85108, and a statewide measure, Sections 85300-85305. Expenditure limitations in both areas appear to be foreclosed by the Buckley decision. For your interest, I am enclosing a copy of the return filed by the commission in Citizens for Jobs and Energy v. Fair Political Practices Commission, an original writ proceeding in the California Supreme Court that challenged the constitutionality of the ballot measure expenditure limitations. In light of the Buckley decision, the Commission is not enforcing the provisions of Sections 85300-85305. The Political Reform Act also limits the amounts that can be spent to circulate signature petitions so that a measure will be placed on the ballot, Sections 85200-85203. These limitations are the subject of litigation currently before the Superior Court of Sacramento County, and we are awaiting judicial determination of the controversy.
